topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, bright and intrusive, right in the middle of my study session. It was from Mr. Harrison. My stomach tightened, a familiar knot forming beneath my ribs. I stared at the screen, my jaw clenching so hard my teeth ached. He had tagged me in a photo from the school event—a photo where I looked utterly miserable, forced into a smile for the camera. A slow, cold burn started in my chest. I didn't swipe it away immediately. Instead, I took a deep, shaky breath, the air catching painfully in my throat. I navigated to his profile, my fingers hovering over the 'unfollow' button. A wave of hot, sharp anger washed over me, making my hands tremble. I finally tapped it, the small action feeling monumental, a tiny assertion of control in a world that felt suddenly too large and too watchful.

[story 2]

I found the evidence tucked away in a forgotten folder on my laptop. A recent post from Ms. Evans, a picture of a rare book she was lecturing about, and right underneath it, a comment: "Still wrestling with Chapter Five, [My Name]." My blood pressure spiked. I felt a restless energy bubbling up, a need to push back against the invisible tether he had cast. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. I walked over to the window, staring out at the dreary rain, my knuckles white where I gripped the sill. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring. I wanted to shout, to confront him directly, but the words caught in my throat, replaced by a simmering, stubborn refusal to simply shrink away.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical blow during lunch break. I was scrolling through my feed, and there, in the middle of a thread about school gossip, was a story posted by Professor Davies. It was a candid shot of him reviewing lesson plans, and his caption mentioned something about 'student engagement.' My breath hitched. A strange, almost defiant stillness settled over me. I didn't move. I just watched the screen, a slow, deliberate movement of my eyes tracing the edges of the image. A tightness constricted my throat, a sensation that felt like iron being slowly squeezed. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the messaging app, not to respond, but just to hold the device, a silent challenge radiating from my posture.
